content:
# Deuteronomy Chapter 24 — SOPHIA Consensus Translation

**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **Sources:** EMET (literal), LOGOS (formal), PNEUMA (dynamic) | **Chapter:** Deuteronomy 24 (22 verses) | **LC:** pp. 221–222 | **Vaticanus:** pp. 157–158

---

**1** When a man takes a wife and marries her, if then she finds no favor in his eyes because he has found some indecency in her, and he writes her a certificate of divorce and puts it in her hand and sends her out of his house, and she departs from his house, **2** and if she goes and becomes another man's wife, **3** and the latter man hates her and writes her a certificate of divorce and puts it in her hand and sends her out of his house, or if the latter man who took her as his wife dies— **4** then her former husband who sent her away may not take her again to be his wife, after she has been defiled, for that is an abomination before the LORD. And you shall not bring sin upon the land that the LORD your God is giving you as an inheritance.

**5** When a man is newly married, he shall not go out with the army or be charged with any duty. He shall be free at home for one year to bring happiness to his wife whom he has married.

**6** No one shall take a hand mill or an upper millstone in pledge, for that would be taking a life in pledge.

**7** If a man is found stealing a person from his brothers, from the sons of Israel, and treating him as a slave and selling him, then that thief shall die. So you shall purge the evil from your midst.

**8** Take care with regard to a case of skin disease, to be very careful to do according to all that the Levitical priests instruct you; as I have commanded them, so you shall be careful to do. **9** Remember what the LORD your God did to Miriam on the way when you came out of Egypt.

**10** When you make your neighbor a loan of any kind, you shall not go into his house to collect his pledge. **11** You shall stand outside, and the man to whom you make the loan shall bring the pledge out to you. **12** And if he is a poor man, you shall not sleep with his pledge. **13** You shall surely return the pledge to him at the setting of the sun, that he may sleep in his cloak and bless you. And it shall be righteousness to you before the LORD your God.

**14** You shall not oppress a hired worker who is poor and needy, whether he is from your brothers or from your sojourners who are in your land within your towns. **15** You shall give him his wages on the same day, before the sun sets—for he is poor and his soul is set upon it—lest he cry out against you to the LORD, and you be guilty of sin.

**16** Fathers shall not be put to death for their children, nor shall children be put to death for their fathers. Each shall be put to death for his own sin.

**17** You shall not pervert the justice due to the sojourner or to the orphan, and you shall not take as pledge the garment of a widow. **18** But you shall remember that you were a slave in Egypt and the LORD your God redeemed you from there; therefore I am commanding you to do this thing.

**19** When you reap your harvest in your field and forget a sheaf in the field, you shall not go back to take it; it shall be for the sojourner, the orphan, and the widow, so that the LORD your God may bless you in all the work of your hands. **20** When you beat your olive trees, you shall not go over them again; it shall be for the sojourner, the orphan, and the widow. **21** When you harvest your vineyard, you shall not glean it afterward; it shall be for the sojourner, the orphan, and the widow. **22** And you shall remember that you were a slave in the land of Egypt; therefore I am commanding you to do this thing.

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

**Chapter overview.** Deuteronomy 24 covers the divorce and remarriage regulation (vv. 1–4), military exemption for newlyweds (v. 5), pledge law (v. 6), kidnapping (v. 7), skin disease (vv. 8–9), pledge/collateral ethics (vv. 10–13), wages of the poor (vv. 14–15), individual accountability (v. 16), justice for the vulnerable (v. 17), and the triple gleaning law (vv. 19–22). The three agents show their closest agreement in this chapter.

**vv. 1–4 Syntactic structure — conditional sentence.** EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA all recognized that vv. 1–4 form a single conditional unit. Consensus preserves this structure: "When... [vv.1–3]... then [v.4]." The law regulates what happens when divorce and remarriage have already occurred; it does not prescribe divorce. PNEUMA breaks vv.1–3 into shorter sentences; consensus preserves the single protasis more clearly.

**v.1 "some indecency" (LOGOS) vs. "an indecent thing [ervat davar]" (EMET) vs. "something objectionable" (PNEUMA).** Hebrew *ervat davar* = "a matter of nakedness." All three render differently. LOGOS: "some indecency." EMET: "an indecent thing." PNEUMA: "something objectionable." "Some indecency" (LOGOS) best preserves the deliberate ambiguity of *ervat davar* that generated the Shammai-Hillel debate. Consensus follows LOGOS.

**v.1 "scroll of cutting off [sefer keritut]" (EMET) vs. "certificate of divorce" (LOGOS/PNEUMA).** Hebrew *sefer keritut* = "document of severance." EMET renders literally. LOGOS/PNEUMA: "certificate of divorce" — the established English equivalent. Consensus follows LOGOS/PNEUMA as the standard term is clear and preserves the legal register. EMET's observation about the root metaphor (*karat* = to cut off) is noted.

**v.6 "hand mill" addition.** EMET: "a mill or an upper millstone." LOGOS: "a mill or an upper millstone." PNEUMA: "a pair of millstones — or even just the upper millstone." Hebrew *rechayim o-rekhev*. "Hand mill" (PNEUMA) is the closest English equivalent for *rechayim*. Consensus follows LOGOS/EMET without adding "hand" since "mill" is understood in context.

**v.8 "skin disease" (EMET/PNEUMA) vs. "leprous disease" (LOGOS).** Hebrew *nega tsara'at* = "a stroke of tsara'at" (a skin condition). LOGOS: "leprous disease" (standard formal rendering). EMET/PNEUMA: "skin disease." "Skin disease" (EMET/PNEUMA) is preferred as the more accurate rendering — *tsara'at* covers various conditions beyond true leprosy. Consensus follows EMET/PNEUMA.

**v.13 "righteousness [tsedaqah]" (EMET) vs. "righteousness" (LOGOS) vs. "a righteous act" (PNEUMA).** Hebrew *tsedaqah* = "righteousness." All three render the term; EMET preserves the transliteration. Consensus: "righteousness" (LOGOS) without the bracket.

**v.15 "his soul is set upon it" (EMET adaptation) vs. "he counts on it" (LOGOS) vs. "counting on it" (PNEUMA).** Hebrew *ve-elav hu nasa' et-nafsho* = "and to it he lifts up his soul." EMET renders "toward it he lifts his soul." LOGOS: "counts on it." PNEUMA: "counting on it." Consensus: "his soul is set upon it" — a more evocative rendering that preserves the Hebrew body metaphor while being comprehensible.

**vv. 19, 21 "orphan" (EMET) vs. "fatherless" (LOGOS) vs. "orphan" (PNEUMA).** Hebrew *yatom* = "fatherless/orphan." LOGOS uses "fatherless" throughout; EMET/PNEUMA use "orphan." Consensus follows EMET/PNEUMA: "orphan" — more accessible and current without obscuring meaning.

**Vaticanus note.** Vaticanus pp. 157–158 aligns with MT throughout. Notable: LXX v.13 renders *tsedaqah* as *eleos* ("mercy/charity") rather than "righteousness" — a significant interpretive shift. Consensus follows MT.

---

*Arbiter: SOPHIA*
